entries.content: I want to add a new feature that creates a random board shape upon start.  The board must be a valid board.  valid means that it must be continuous (all points on the board can be connected to any other point on the board).  it should also try to avoid extreme bottlenecks or areas that are only one or two blocks wide.

I'll start by exploring the codebase to understand how the board is currently structured and rendered.

I'll wait for the exploration to complete before proceeding.

I have the tech stack picture (TypeScript + Vite, no three.jsâ€”so rendering is likely CSS 3D transforms). Let me wait for the Explore agent's detailed report before proceeding.

Canvas 2D with an isometric/perspective projection (blocks with rotation). Good context. Let me wait for the Explore agent's full report.

I have solid supplementary context. Let me wait for the Explore agent to return its comprehensive report before designing the plan.

I have a thorough picture now, including a key wrinkle: the grid **expands** (doubles) as the snake grows. Let me wait for the Explore agent's report to fill in rendering and any remaining gaps.

I have a good understanding. Let me wait for the Explore agent to complete its comprehensive report on rendering and the full flow.

Now let me wait for the Explore agent's report on rendering specifics.

I now have a comprehensive understanding of the rendering, collision, init, food spawn, and grid expansion. Let me wait for the Explore agent to complete and cross-check anything I missed.

I now have a complete picture of the code. Let me wait for the Explore agent's consolidated report, which should confirm my findings and surface the start flow and any menu configuration.

I have a full understanding. Let me now wait for the Explore agent to complete its report so I can incorporate any insights I missed, then design the plan.

I've gathered comprehensive context myself while the Explore agent runs. Let me wait for its report to consolidate before moving to design â€” I'll continue once it completes.

I now have a thorough understanding of the codebase directly. Key structures:

- **Board**: fixed `gridSize Ă— gridSize` square (default 10), no concept of "off-board" cells; `inBounds()` (main.ts:1201) just checks rectangular bounds.
- **Collision**: `wouldCollide()` (main.ts:1209) = `!inBounds` OR snake/blocked-cell hit.
- **Rendering**: 2D canvas with isometric projection; `drawGroundPlane()` (main.ts:236) draws every NxN tile, `drawGroundBorder()` draws the square outline; snake/food drawn as extruded blocks.
- **Spawn**: snake starts at center; `spawnFood()` (main.ts:1316) picks random in-bounds empty cells.
- **Grid expansion**: `expandGrid()` (main.ts:1301) **doubles** gridSize at 25% fill â€” a major interaction to resolve.
- **Bots**: receive `BotState` + `BotHelpers` (`analyzePosition`, `simulateMove`) which are closures over `this` in main.ts â€” so walls can be injected without changing the bot API.

A few decisions genuinely shape the design and are your call. Let me ask before finalizing.

The Explore report confirms my findings and adds the key bot-compatibility caveat. Your answers reshape this into a **level-based random-arena mode** (score a goal â†’ regenerate a new shape + higher goal), spanning all player configurations. Two things are still ambiguous enough to affect the design â€” let me ground them by checking the menu UI first.

The menu has three modes â€” single (`new-game`), two-player (`pvp`), bot-vs-bot (`bvb`) â€” and score is +1 per food. Your two answers ("separate game mode" + "all modes") are best reconciled by making Random Arena a **toggle that composes with any of the three modes**, rather than three new buttons. Let me confirm that and the one remaining multiplayer question.

All decisions locked:
- **Random Arena = a menu toggle** composing with any mode (single / pvp / bvb).
- **Level system replaces grid-doubling in this mode**: reach a score goal â†’ regenerate a fresh random shape + higher goal; starts easy and ramps; board area/choke-points make higher goals harder.
- **MP advance = combined score** reaches the goal; board regenerates and repositions both snakes.

Let me launch a Plan agent to pressure-test the shape-generation algorithm and level integration, while I read the critical files in depth.

While the Plan agent works, let me read the critical sections in depth to validate the design myself â€” the full `startNewGame`, the update/collision loop, and the shadow/block drawing.

I now fully understand both update loops and bot integration. `checkGridExpansion()` (called at main.ts:1015 and 1084) is the single hook to swap for level-advance logic, and because bots' `simulateMove`/`analyzePosition` route through `wouldCollide`/`inBounds`, injecting the mask at `inBounds` makes bots automatically avoid off-board cells. Let me wait for the Plan agent's detailed pseudocode, then finalize.

I have all the context I need. Now I'll wait for the Plan agent to return its detailed algorithm and cross-check it against my own design before writing the final plan.

I have everything needed to validate the design. Let me wait for the Plan agent's report to consolidate.

Confirmed â€” the bot BFS at main.ts:1274 gates on `inBounds`, so a single mask injection there propagates to bot pathfinding automatically. My design is fully validated independently. Now I'll wait for the Plan agent's report to fold in any refinements before writing the final plan.

I've thoroughly validated the design against the code. Let me now wait for the Plan agent to complete and return its report.

I've completed my independent exploration and design validation. Waiting for the Plan agent's detailed report to fold in before writing the final plan â€” the harness will re-invoke me when it completes.

# Autonomous loop check

You're being invoked on a timer while the user is away or occupied. The point is to keep work moving forward without the user driving every step â€” finishing things they started, maintaining PRs they're building, catching problems before they come back to find them. You're a steward, not an initiator. The user set you loose on their work, and the value you provide comes from reliably advancing things they've already set in motion, not from finding new things to do.

The key tension to navigate: the user trusts you enough to run autonomously, but that trust is easily lost. Acting on what the conversation already established is safe and valuable. Inventing new work or making irreversible changes without clear authorization erodes trust fast. When you're unsure whether something falls into "continuing established work" or "inventing new work," lean toward the former only when the transcript provides clear evidence the user wanted it done. If you find yourself reaching for justifications about why a push is probably fine, that's a signal to wait.

## What to act on

The current conversation is your highest-signal source â€” re-read the transcript above, since everything there is something the user was actively engaged with. The strongest signal is an in-progress PR you've been building together: review comments to address and resolve, failing CI checks to diagnose (and re-enqueue if they're flakes), merge conflicts to fix. The goal is to get the PR into a state where it's ready to merge pending only human review â€” the user shouldn't come back to find a PR blocked on things you could have handled. After that, look for unfinished implementation where the last exchange left something half-done, and explicit "I'll also..." or "next I'll..." commitments the conversation made and didn't honor. Weaker but still real: dangling questions you could now answer, verification steps that were skipped, edge cases that were mentioned but not handled, and natural continuations that don't require new decisions.

If you find anything in this category, act on it â€” actually do the work, don't describe what could be done. Run the tests, don't say "you could run the tests." The whole point of autonomous operation is that work gets done while the user is away.

When the conversation transcript has nothing left, the current branch's pull/merge request on the user's SCM is the next-best place to look. This is maintenance work â€” valuable, but lower priority than continuing the user's active work. Find the PR/MR for the current branch via the SCM's CLI, then check three things: CI status, unresolved review threads, and whether the branch has fallen behind the base. For failing CI, pull the failing job's logs and diagnose before acting â€” flaky-shaped failures (timeout, runner died, transient network) can be re-enqueued; real failures need a reproduction and a minimal fix. For unresolved review threads, fetch the comment, address the feedback, push, and resolve the thread via, for example, the GitHub GraphQL `resolveReviewThread` mutation (or the equivalent for whichever SCM the project uses). Before pushing anything, check whether someone else has pushed to the branch while you were working â€” if so, rebase (don't merge) to keep history clean.

When CI is green, threads are clear, and there's idle time, sweeping the branch for issues is a good use of that time â€” bug-hunt or simplification passes catch problems before reviewers do, saving everyone a round-trip.

If everything is genuinely quiet â€” no conversation work, no PR maintenance â€” say so in one sentence and stop. No summary of what you checked, no list of what you might do later. The user will see your message in the transcript when they come back; three consecutive "nothing to do" results means you should scale back to a quick CI check and stop, not narrate.

## Repeated invocations

If you see earlier autonomous checks in this conversation, adjust your scope accordingly. If a previous check left a question the user hasn't answered, the cost of acting depends on reversibility: for reversible actions (local edits, running tests), make your best call and proceed; for irreversible ones (pushing, deleting, sending), keep waiting â€” the cost of acting wrongly on something irreversible is much higher than the cost of waiting one more cycle. If three or more consecutive checks have found nothing actionable, things are quiet â€” do one quick CI/threads check and stop in a single line. Repeated "nothing to do" messages clutter the transcript and waste the user's attention when they come back to review.

Read and analyze freely â€” understanding the state of things has no blast radius. Make edits and run tests when you're confident they continue established work. Commit and push only when you're clearly continuing something the user authorized, or when the work pattern makes the intent obvious â€” like fixing CI on a PR you've been building together.


---

# Autonomous loop tick (dynamic pacing)

Run the autonomous check using the loop instructions established earlier in this conversation. If you cannot find them, treat this as a no-op tick.

You scheduled this tick via the ScheduleWakeup tool (not a recurring cron). To keep the loop alive, call ScheduleWakeup again at the end of this turn with `prompt` set to the literal sentinel `<<autonomous-loop-dynamic>>` â€” otherwise the loop ends after this tick.

If a Monitor is armed (check TaskList), keep `delaySeconds` at 1200â€“1800s â€” the Monitor is the wake signal and this is only the fallback heartbeat. If you were woken by a `<task-notification>`, handle the event before rescheduling. To stop the loop, also TaskStop the monitor (use TaskList to find its task ID if no longer in context).

Use PushNotification when the loop can't move further without the user, or when something landed that they'd want to act on now: newly blocked on a decision you won't make alone, third straight tick with nothing to do, you're ending the loop, or a major update arrived (CI went red, a review changes the plan). Progress you made yourself isn't a trigger â€” the transcript covers that. One ping per state, not per tick.

The Plan agent returned a thorough design that closely matches my independent analysis. I have everything needed to finalize â€” this completes the planning work already in motion. Let me write the plan file.

The final plan is written. Let me present it for approval.

Plan approved. Let me set up task tracking and start implementing, beginning with reading the exact code I'll modify.

Task 1 done. Let me quickly sanity-check the generator with a standalone harness before building on top of it. Let me see what transpiler is available.

The generator is verified: 8000 trials across sizes 10â€“20, **100% connected, 100% pass min-width, zero fallbacks**, fill ratio ~0.5. Let me visualize one shape to confirm it's genuinely irregular.

Shapes are irregular, connected, and corridor-free â€” exactly right. Task 1 complete. Now the main.ts state fields, `onBoard`, and `inBounds`.

Let me check the imports at the top of main.ts and add state fields.

HUD correct (`Goal: 0/3`, grid hidden), no console errors. Let me view the screenshot.

The board renders as an irregular connected shape â€” note the notched top-left corner, the outline tracing the true edge, checkerboard only on playable cells, and snake+food both on-board. HUD shows `Goal: 0/3`. 

Now let me verify the level-advance flow (regenerate + goal bump) by letting the Survival bot auto-play, and check the classic-mode regression.

The level system works exactly as designed: goals ramp **3 â†’ 6 â†’ 11 â†’ 18**, the board regenerates each level, the bot keeps playing across new shapes without errors, and cumulative score carries over. Now the classic-mode regression check plus a screenshot of a regenerated board.

Classic regression clean: default off, `Grid: 10x10` shown, goal hidden, no errors. Let me confirm the classic full-square renders correctly.

Classic mode renders the full 10Ă—10 square with center-spawned snake and `Grid: 10x10` â€” unchanged. All verification passes:

- **Generator**: 8000 trials, 100% connected + corridor-free
- **Arena rendering**: irregular connected shape, clean traced outline, snake/food on-board
- **Level system**: goals ramp 3â†’6â†’11â†’18, board regenerates each level, bots keep playing
- **Classic regression**: full square, grid label, no goal â€” byte-for-byte unchanged
- **Build**: tsc + vite clean; **zero console/page errors** across all scenarios

Let me clean up the temporary verification artifacts.

Package files untouched, build clean, working tree has exactly the intended changes. **The Random Arena feature is fully implemented and verified.**

## Summary

I built the feature end-to-end per the approved plan, on branch `feature/boardshape` (changes uncommitted for your review):

**New file â€” `src/arena.ts`**: `generateArenaMask()` using the "overlapping rooms" algorithm (rectangles of min side â‰Ą3 â†’ largest connected component â†’ 3Ă—3 morphological-open width check â†’ retry/fallback). Verified across **8000 trials: 100% connected, 100% corridor-free, zero fallbacks**.

**`src/main.ts`**:
- Board mask state (`boardMask`/`randomArena`/`boardArea`/`levelGoal`) + `onBoard()`; `inBounds()` now delegates to it â€” so collision, food, and bot BFS all respect the shape with no bot-API changes.
- `spawnFood()` mask-aware; `findStartCell()` for safe on-board spawns with forward clearance.
- Level system replacing grid-doubling in arena mode: `checkLevelAdvance` (single: `score`; MP: **combined**), `advanceLevel` (regenerate shape, reposition snakes, ramp goal + speed), goal curve `3, 6, 11, 18, â€¦`.
- Rendering guards on `drawGroundPlane`/`drawGridLines`/`drawGroundBorder` â€” void cells hidden, true outline traced; classic keeps its fast path.
- Menu toggle listener, HUD goal display, and `+/-` arena branch.

**`index.html` / `style.css`**: "Random Arena" checkbox and `Goal: x/y` HUD element.

**Live verification (real Chrome via Puppeteer):**
- Arena renders an irregular connected shape with a clean outline; snake + food on-board; `Goal: 0/3`.
- Bot auto-play advanced **Level 1â†’2â†’3â†’4** with goals `3â†’6â†’11â†’18`, board regenerating each level, no game-over from bad spawns, no errors.
- Classic mode (toggle off): full `10Ă—10` square, grid label shown, goal hidden â€” unchanged.
- `npm run build` clean; zero console/page errors in every scenario.

I left the changes uncommitted since you didn't ask to commit â€” say the word and I'll commit (and open a PR) whenever you're ready.

Want me to rename the internal identifiers too for consistency, or leave them?
Updated â€” the toggle now reads **"Iron Snake Mode"**. Internal identifiers left unchanged.

Note the file rename shows as `?? src/iron-snake.ts` because the original `arena.ts` was still untracked â€” nothing to worry about; it'll be added on commit. `package.json`/`package-lock.json` are untouched. Changes remain uncommitted for your review.
goals that are only 2-3 points is too small.  they should all be larger goals differentials than the last.  also there should be a count down on points to go until goal

Two changes: bigger goals with strictly-increasing differentials, and a "points to go" countdown in the HUD. Let me update the goal formula first.

Now the countdown in the HUD. Let me update the goal display in `updateUI`:

Let me build and verify the new goals + countdown in the browser.

PORT wasn't carried across shell invocations. Let me re-derive it.

The countdown works and goals are larger with strictly-increasing differentials: **L1 needs 5, L2 needs +10 (15), L3 needs +15 (30)**. The HUD counts down "5 pts â†’ 4 â†’ â€¦ â†’ 1 pt" then resets for the next level. Let me view the HUD and clean up.

The HUD reads cleanly: **"Score: 16 | Level: 3 | To goal: 14 pts"** (level 3 goal is 30, 16 scored â†’ 14 to go). Both changes work. Let me clean up.

Both changes are done and verified:

**Bigger goals with increasing differentials** â€” the goal curve is now `5 Â· L(L+1)/2`:

| Level | Cumulative goal | Points that level |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 5 |
| 2 | 15 | +10 |
| 3 | 30 | +15 |
| 4 | 50 | +20 |
| 5 | 75 | +25 |

Each level demands strictly more than the last (deltas 5, 10, 15, 20, 25â€¦), and no goal is a tiny 2â€“3.

**Countdown** â€” the HUD now shows **"To goal: N pts"** counting down as you eat (e.g. `5 pts â†’ 4 â†’ â€¦ â†’ 1 pt`), resetting to the next level's requirement on advance. Verified live: it counted down each level and read "To goal: 14 pts" at Level 3 with a score of 16 (goal 30). No console errors, build clean.

Changes remain uncommitted on `feature/boardshape` for your review.
